title: GPT 5.6: A Comprehensive Review of OpenAI's Latest Frontier Models
summary:
OpenAI's GPT 5.6 represents a significant leap in AI capabilities, introducing three distinct models: GPT 5.6 Sol (the largest and most performant), Terra (mid-tier), and Luna (the smallest and fastest). This latest family of models excels in complex, multi-step tasks, particularly when used with agentic frameworks like the redesigned ChatGPT desktop app (formerly Codex). The models demonstrate impressive proficiency in generating web applications, simulating physics, creating music, rendering 3D scenes, and producing detailed research reports with minimal user intervention.

However, GPT 5.6 is not without its limitations. While it outperforms competitors like Claude Opus and Gemini in several benchmarks, including agentic reasoning and long-horizon tasks, it exhibits a higher hallucination rate. Additionally, its performance in specialized tasks, such as identifying medical conditions or hidden objects in images, remains inconsistent. The models are available via the ChatGPT platform, desktop app, and API, with varying access levels for free and paid users.
key_takeaways: GPT 5.6 consists of three models: Sol (most performant), Terra (mid-tier), and Luna (fastest but least intelligent).
The models excel in complex tasks like web app development, physics simulations, music creation, and 3D rendering with minimal user input.
GPT 5.6 is designed for agentic use, enabling autonomous workflows with tools like Gemini TTS and Hyperframes.
Benchmarks show GPT 5.6 Sol outperforms competitors like Claude Opus and Fable in agentic reasoning, long-horizon tasks, and cost efficiency.
GPT 5.6 has a higher hallucination rate compared to Claude Fable and open-source models like GLM 5.2.
The models are available via ChatGPT online, the free desktop app (Codex), and API, with paid users accessing higher effort levels and all three models.
GPT 5.6 demonstrates emergent abilities, such as learning new patterns on the fly, as evidenced by its performance in Arc AGI 2 and 3 benchmarks.

sections.heading: Introduction to GPT 5.6
sections.body: OpenAI has released GPT 5.6, its latest and most advanced family of AI models, comprising three variants: GPT 5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna. Sol is the largest and most performant model, designed for complex tasks, while Terra offers a balance between performance and speed, and Luna is the smallest and fastest but less intelligent. These models are capable of handling routine tasks like writing emails or summarizing documents, but their true potential lies in tackling more challenging, multi-step workflows. To fully leverage GPT 5.6, OpenAI recommends using it with an agentic framework, such as the redesigned ChatGPT desktop app (formerly known as Codex), which allows for multi-agent collaboration, file and folder management, and automation scheduling.

sections.heading: Complex Task Performance
sections.body:
GPT 5.6 demonstrates remarkable capabilities in executing complex tasks with minimal user intervention. For instance, it can create a fully functional web application featuring an interactive anime avatar with real-time voice chat, using tools like GPT Image for animations and Google's Gemini for voice synthesis. The model also excels in physics simulations, generating a visually stunning liquid splash simulation with adjustable gravity, lighting, and hand-tracking controls—all coded from scratch without relying on external libraries.

In creative domains, GPT 5.6 can produce a digital audio workstation (DAW) interface with multiple instruments, effects, and automation, generating a coherent 32-bar song. While the output may not match professional human standards, it surpasses results from other frontier models. Additionally, the model can render 3D scenes and create intricate math animations, such as Fourier epicycles drawing a butterfly, though these tasks often require iterative prompting to achieve satisfactory results.

sections.heading: Agentic Workflows and Tool Integration
sections.body:
GPT 5.6 is optimized for agentic workflows, enabling it to autonomously call and use various tools to complete tasks. For example, it can create a promotional video for ByteDance's C-Dream 5 Pro by integrating Gemini TTS for voiceovers and Hyperframes for animations, even installing and configuring the tools independently. The model can also generate comprehensive presentations, such as a slideshow comparing the Q1 2026 earnings reports of Alphabet, Nvidia, and Amazon, synthesizing data and providing insightful conclusions.

The ChatGPT Work tab further enhances its agentic capabilities by allowing users to link files and folders from platforms like Slack, GitHub, and Google Drive, enabling autonomous workflows for tasks like data analysis, presentation creation, and information synthesis.

sections.heading: Benchmarks and Performance
sections.body:
GPT 5.6 Sol outperforms competitors like Claude Opus and Fable in several benchmarks, particularly in agentic reasoning and long-horizon tasks. For example, it achieves the highest score in the Agentic Last Exam benchmark while being significantly more cost-efficient. In the Agentic Decoding benchmark, GPT 5.6 Sol at max effort surpasses Claude Fable, which is both less performant and more expensive. The model also excels in genomics tasks, as demonstrated by its performance in GeneBench, where it far outpaces Claude Opus.

However, GPT 5.6 Sol exhibits a higher hallucination rate (89%) compared to Claude Fable (55%) and GLM 5.2 (28%), as measured by the Omniscient benchmark. Despite this, it ranks first in DeepSuite's leaderboard for long-horizon software engineering tasks, though its confidence intervals overlap with Claude Fable 5. Notably, GPT 5.6 Sol demonstrates emergent abilities, scoring 92.5% in Arc AGI 2 and nearly 8% in Arc AGI 3, showcasing its capacity to learn new patterns on the fly.

sections.heading: Limitations and Challenges
sections.body:
Despite its strengths, GPT 5.6 has notable limitations. It struggles with tasks requiring precise visual identification, such as finding hidden animals in images or accurately identifying medical conditions like tumors in scans. While it can generate detailed research reports, such as a technical analysis of leukemia's molecular drivers, its outputs in creative tasks like video production often lack professional polish, with issues like overlapping text and images.

The model's high hallucination rate is a significant drawback, particularly for tasks requiring factual accuracy. Additionally, GPT 5.6 Sol, especially in ultra or max effort modes, is considerably slower than other models, often taking 15-30 minutes to complete complex tasks.

sections.heading: Access and Availability
sections.body: GPT 5.6 is rolling out across multiple platforms. On the ChatGPT online platform, only paid users currently have access to the models. However, the ChatGPT desktop app (Codex) is free to download and offers access to GPT 5.6 for all users, though free users are limited to the medium Terra model. Paid users can access all three models (Sol, Terra, and Luna) and adjust the effort level up to Ultra. GPT 5.6 is also available via API, making it accessible for developers and enterprises.

faq.question: What are the different models in GPT 5.6?
faq.answer: GPT 5.6 consists of three models: Sol (the largest and most performant), Terra (mid-tier), and Luna (the smallest and fastest but least intelligent).

faq.question: How can I access GPT 5.6?
faq.answer: GPT 5.6 is available via the ChatGPT online platform (paid users only), the free ChatGPT desktop app (Codex), and the API. Free users of the desktop app have access to the Terra model, while paid users can access all three models and adjust effort levels.

faq.question: What tasks can GPT 5.6 perform?
faq.answer: GPT 5.6 can handle complex tasks such as developing web applications, simulating physics, creating music, rendering 3D scenes, generating research reports, and producing promotional videos. It is optimized for agentic workflows, enabling autonomous tool integration and multi-step task completion.

faq.question: How does GPT 5.6 compare to other models like Claude Opus and Fable?
faq.answer: GPT 5.6 Sol outperforms Claude Opus and Fable in benchmarks like agentic reasoning, long-horizon tasks, and cost efficiency. However, it has a higher hallucination rate (89%) compared to Claude Fable (55%) and struggles with tasks requiring precise visual identification.

faq.question: What are the limitations of GPT 5.6?
faq.answer: GPT 5.6 has a high hallucination rate, struggles with visual identification tasks, and can produce unpolished outputs in creative tasks like video production. It is also slower in ultra or max effort modes, often taking 15-30 minutes to complete complex tasks.

faq.question: Can GPT 5.6 learn new patterns on the fly?
faq.answer: Yes, GPT 5.6 demonstrates emergent abilities to learn new patterns, as evidenced by its performance in Arc AGI 2 (92.5%) and Arc AGI 3 (nearly 8%) benchmarks.
